---
title: "Books that made me switch fields, ranked"
description: "The reading list that turned a 30-year software career into an MRes in biomedicine."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [books, career]
draft: true
---

*[Scaffold — fill in. This post would link out to the deep-dive posts on Mukherjee, Lane, Dawkins, and Pinker.]*

The interest in biology built slowly, then suddenly. Looking back, a handful of books did most of the work. Ranked here by influence on the decision to switch fields — not by quality, not by chronology.

## 1. *The Emperor of All Maladies* — Siddhartha Mukherjee

*[The tipping point. Why this one rather than the others. Link to deep-dive post.]*

## 2. *The Vital Question* — Nick Lane

*[The framework. The energy-first view of biology. Link to deep-dive post.]*

## 3. *The Selfish Gene* — Richard Dawkins

*[The lens — replicator vs vehicle, the information frame on inheritance. Link to deep-dive post.]*

## 4. *[TBD]*

*[Candidates: Ridley's *Genome*; Lane's *Power, Sex, Suicide*; Nessa Carey's *The Epigenetics Revolution*; Mukherjee's *The Gene*. Pick the one that earned its place.]*

## 5. *[TBD]*

*[Wildcard slot — something less obvious.]*

## Honourable mentions

*[Books that were good but didn't quite move the needle for this particular decision. Maybe: textbooks (Molecular Biology of the Cell), MOOC companions, Ridley.]*

## What I'd skip

*[Books that have a reputation but didn't repay the time investment for me. Be honest; be brief.]*

## The MOOC layer

*[The reading wasn't enough on its own. MITx Introduction to Biology (Eric Lander) was the bridge. Brief note on which courses moved the dial.]*
